title: Library, information and archive services assistant
change: Occupational standard and assessment plan revised.
overviewOfRole: Helping people using places like libraries and archives find the information and resources they need.

knowledges.detail: The organisation in which they work, its relationship with stakeholders or partners, its products and services.
Team-working and how this contributes to a successful service.
The regulations regarding information use. For example copyright, intellectual property, licensing and data protection.
The process of information retrieval for staff to help service users access resources and services.
The organisation's process of acquiring, locating, maintaining and disposing of resources. For example, books, journals, records and artefacts.
The concept of digital continuity ensuring original records are preserved as required.
The specific features of archives, media and their legal and historical characteristics.
The changing nature of collections in archives and libraries and organisational policies relating to collections management and development decisions.
The use of collaborative and communicative tools and activities. For example, shared storage drives and the organisation’s social media channels.

Information and digital literacy frameworks and how they could be applied in the workplace. For example, Media and Information Literacy Alliance (MILA  framework) or Society of College, National and University Libraries (SONCUL).
The role that information professionals and services play in developing knowledge and literacy. For example, reading, writing, numeracy, digital, health and general cultural enrichment.
The capabilities of digital technologies, AI, library and content management systems to store, organise and search for information.
Different users' needs when looking for information, how they go about finding it, and how various information services are designed to meet those different needs.
Methods for promoting services and collections to service users.
The ways in which organisations make their services more accessible to users. For example, depending on their age, disability or ethnicity.
Listening and questioning techniques and how they are applied.
Different learning activities and the importance of tailoring activities to specific audiences.
Different communication skills and strategies to maximise understanding for individuals.
The impact of online environments and physical spaces on the provision of services to different service users.
The process for evaluating existing services against benchmarks, standards or customer needs.

behaviours.detail: Practises in an ethical and legal manner by acting with integrity, honesty and accountability and protecting confidentiality.
Supports an inclusive culture and treat colleagues and external stakeholders fairly and with respect.
Supports the needs and concerns of others within the workplace and service users.
Seeks to conserve and preserve our information heritage in all formats.
Supports the implementation of environmental and sustainability practices.

skills.detail: Communicate information to stakeholders whilst adhering to organisational policies and procedures.
Use information management processes to store, manage and retrieve records and data in accordance with the organisation’s policy and practices.
Describe and arrange material or resources, by applying identified cataloguing standards in order to create online catalogues and other finding aids to meet users’ needs.
Employ information retrieval techniques. For example, searching online databases, catalogues or electronic document record management systems.
Perform analogue, physical, or digital preservation practices to keep collections safe in accordance with institutional or sector guidelines.
Use listening and questioning techniques to clarify and meet users’ information requirements and manage expectations. For example, by signposting to alternative resources and services.
Develop users' information, reading and digital skills to help them identify, find, access and evaluate information.
Contribute to learning activities for specific audiences.

Use promotion techniques for resources so that users and potential users are aware of their value, impact and benefit. For example, by curating collections and displays in effective ways, undertaking outreach activities.
Assess online environments or physical spaces, anticipating user needs. For example, re-organising study or virtual spaces, suggesting improvements to catalogues or web pages.
Achieve goals by working with internal and external stakeholders and partners.
Use effective communication skills. For example, oral, written, presentation or active listening.

reviewDetails: this apprenticeship will be reviewed in accordance with our change request policy.
clusterDescription: Co-ordination, management and delivery of specific business processes and also their direct provision to users

professionalRecognition.bodyName: Chartered Institute of Library and Information Professionals
professionalRecognition.partialFreeText: This standard can be used to contribute to an application for professional recognition.

professionalRecognition.level: Foundation Member of the Archives and Records Association (FMARA)
professionalRecognition.partialFreeText: This standard can be used to contribute to an application for professional recognition.

duties.dutyDetail: Implement the organisational policies for handling information, physical and digital collections and related resources; using initiative to interpret users’ needs and solve their problems within legislative frameworks.
Acquire, receive, record, circulate and develop collections of physical and digital resources.
Provide access to information and related sources to meet users’ diverse requirements.
Support users to develop confidence and skills in reading, researching, understanding and evaluating information.
Promote content, collections and displays to highlight value and impact and sustain user interest.
Administer and support the management and development of collections.
Administer the withdrawal of information and related resources from use and store or dispose of them safely and sustainably.
Contribute to the creation of an environment that supports the development of users and encourages learning and exploration of information and related resources.
Work with colleagues to support ways in which your organisation collaborates with users and stakeholders in the shaping, development and innovation of the service.
Deliver excellent customer service to all users to support communities for sharing and engaging with information and related resources.
Catalogue and organise information and collections of physical and digital resources.
Store and preserve information and collections safely and securely.
